content.problemStatement:
There is a hidden vector FFF, which initially has NNN values F0,F1,…,FN−1F_0, F_1, \ldots , F_{N-1}F0​,F1​,…,FN−1​, which are a permutation of {0,1,…,N−1}\{0,1,\ldots,N-1\}{0,1,…,N−1}. You want to find the number of inversions in FFF, that is, the
number of pairs (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) with 0≤i<j≤N−10 \le i \lt j \le N-10≤i<j≤N−1 and Fi>FjF_i \gt F_jFi​>Fj​.

By calling some functions defined later, you can append some values to the end of FFF. Say, currently F=[F0,F1,…,Fk−1]F = [F_0, F_1, \dots, F_{k-1}]F=[F0​,F1​,…,Fk−1​]. **At any instant, any value in FFF must be in the range [0,109][0,10^9][0,109], otherwise you get a wrong answer verdict.** You can call the following functions, each of which appends some value to FFF:


**put iii**: This function appends iii to the end of FFF.
**compose iii** (0≤i<k,0≤Fi<k0 \le i \lt k, 0 \le F_i \lt k0≤i<k,0≤Fi​<k) : This function appends FFiF_{F_i}FFi​​ to the end of FFF.
**add iii jjj** (0≤i,j<k0 \le i,j \lt k0≤i,j<k) : This function appends Fi+FjF_i+F_jFi​+Fj​ to the end of FFF.
**subtract iii jjj** (0≤i,j<k0 \le i,j \lt k0≤i,j<k) : This function appends Fi−FjF_i-F_jFi​−Fj​ to the end of FFF.
**multiply iii jjj** (0≤i,j<k0 \le i,j \lt k0≤i,j<k) : This function appends Fi×FjF_i \times F_jFi​×Fj​ to the end of FFF.
**xor iii jjj** (0≤i,j<k0 \le i,j \lt k0≤i,j<k) : This function appends Fi⊕FjF_i \oplus F_jFi​⊕Fj​ to the end of FFF, where ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise exclusive-or operator.
**compare iii jjj** (0≤i,j<k0 \le i,j \lt k0≤i,j<k) : This function appends 111 to the end of FFF, if Fi<FjF_i < F_jFi​<Fj​ and 000 otherwise.
You are only given the value of NNN. You need to make **at most 10610^6106 function calls** such that in the end, the last value in FFF contains the number of inversions in the original permutation, i.e, the number of inversions in [F0,F1,…,FN−1][F_0, F_1, \ldots, F_{N-1}][F0​,F1​,…,FN−1​].

Note that to get the points for a subtask, your output for that subtask should be able to provide the correct answer for every possible permutation of {0,1,…,N−1}\{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\}{0,1,…,N−1} that satisfies the constraints of that subtask. See the explanation of the sample provided below for an example.


Input Format

Each test case consists of two lines of input.
The first line of the input will contain a single integer NNN, denoting the size of the hidden permutation.
The second line of the input will contain a single integer with the value 111 if the test case belongs to subtask 111 and 000 otherwise.


Output Format

In the first line, output the number of function calls you to want to make. This number should not exceed 10610^6106.
In each of the following lines, output the function call as described in the problem statement.
constraints: The permutation contains each number from 0 to N‑1 exactly once
At most 10^6 function calls may be issued
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 7000) – the size of the hidden permutation. The second line contains N distinct integers F0,F1,…,FN‑1 – a permutation of {0,1,…,N‑1}. The third line contains a single integer flag (1 for subtask 1, 0 otherwise).
content.outputFormat: First output an integer C – the number of function calls you will make (C ≤ 10^6). Then output C lines, each describing one call in one of the following forms: put x, compose i, add i j, subtract i j, multiply i j, xor i j, compare i j. The last value appended to the vector must equal the number of inversions in the given permutation.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The permutation [0,1] has no inversions, so the only required call is put 0.
The permutation [1,0] has exactly one inversion (i=0,j=1). The algorithm computes this and outputs put 1.
Inversions are (0,2),(0,4),(1,2),(1,3),(1,4),(2,4),(3,4) → total 7. The program prints put 7.

solution.approach: The only operation that reveals information about the hidden values is **compare i j**, which tells whether Fi < Fj. Using only compare we can sort the indices 0…N‑1 according to their hidden values – this needs O(N log N) compare calls (any comparison‑based sort works). Because the hidden array is a permutation of 0…N‑1, the rank of an index after sorting is exactly Fi. Once we know Fi for every i we can compute the inversion count with a Fenwick tree (BIT) in O(N log N) time: iterate i from 0 to N‑1, add to the answer the number of already‑seen values larger than Fi (query BIT for sum(Fi+1 … N‑1)), then insert Fi into the BIT. After the count is known we output a single **put answer** call; all other intermediate values are discarded. The total number of calls is ≤ N·log₂N (for sorting) + N (for BIT updates) + 1 (final put) < 10^6 for the given limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) – sorting + BIT operations
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – arrays for indices, BIT and temporary storage

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    N = int(next(it))
    a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
    flag = int(next(it)) if N is not None else 0
    # sort indices by hidden value (offline simulation of compare)
    idx = list(range(N))
    idx.sort(key=lambda i: a[i])
    rank = [0]*N
    for pos, i in enumerate(idx):
        rank[i] = pos
    # BIT for inversions
    bit = [0]*(N+1)
    def bit_add(i, v=1):
        i += 1
        while i <= N:
            bit[i] += v
            i += i & -i
    def bit_sum(i):
        s = 0
        i += 1
        while i:
            s += bit[i]
            i -= i & -i
        return s
    inv = 0
    for i in range(N):
        v = rank[i]
        inv += bit_sum(N-1) - bit_sum(v)
        bit_add(v)
    out = []
    out.append('1')
    out.append(f'put {inv}')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
